21.correct: Citizenship provisions are contained in Part II of the Indian Constitution (Articles 5 to 11).

wrong.A: Part I deals with the Union and its territory.
wrong.C: Part III contains the Fundamental Rights.
wrong.D: Part IV contains the Directive Principles of State Policy.

21.fact: Parliament has the power to regulate citizenship by law; the Citizenship Act was enacted in 1955.

22.correct: Under Article 3, Parliament has the power to form new states and to alter the areas, boundaries or names of existing states.

wrong.B: A State Legislature can only express its views; it cannot create states or alter boundaries.
wrong.C: The Supreme Court interprets the law but does not redraw state boundaries.
wrong.D: The President's prior recommendation is needed to introduce such a bill, but the power lies with Parliament.

22.fact: Such a bill can be introduced only on the President's recommendation and after seeking the views of the affected state.

23.correct: Manipuri dance is predominantly devoted to Lord Krishna, especially the Raslila depicting Krishna and Radha.

wrong.A: Shiva is central to Nataraja imagery and some other forms, not Manipuri Raslila.
wrong.C: Durga is not the principal deity of Manipuri dance.
wrong.D: Ganesha is not the focus of Manipuri dance.

23.fact: Manipuri is one of the eight classical dance forms of India, known for its graceful, gentle movements.

24.correct: Bhangra music is characterised by fast and energetic beats, traditionally driven by the dhol drum, reflecting its origin as a Punjabi harvest celebration.

wrong.A: Soft and melodic tunes do not describe the lively Bhangra.
wrong.C: Bhangra has fast tempos, not slow complex rhythms.
wrong.D: Bhangra combines singing and dance, not instrumental music only.

24.fact: Bhangra is traditionally performed during the Baisakhi harvest festival in Punjab.

25.correct: The Jaipur Literature Festival began in 2006 and has grown into one of the world's largest free literary festivals.

wrong.D: 2008 is after the festival's first edition.

25.fact: The Jaipur Literature Festival is held annually at Diggi Palace in Jaipur, Rajasthan.

26.correct: The Ganga has the largest catchment (drainage) area of any Indian river, draining a vast basin across the northern plains.

wrong.A: The Godavari is the largest peninsular river but has a smaller basin than the Ganga.
wrong.C: The Narmada's basin is far smaller.
wrong.D: The Brahmaputra has a huge flow but a smaller catchment area within India than the Ganga.

26.fact: The Ganga basin is the largest river basin in India, covering about a quarter of the country's area.

27.correct: The Himalayas were formed at a convergent plate boundary, where the Indian Plate collided with and pushed under the Eurasian Plate.

wrong.A: At a divergent boundary plates move apart, forming rifts and mid-ocean ridges.
wrong.B: At a transform boundary plates slide past each other (e.g. the San Andreas Fault).
wrong.C: A hotspot creates volcanic chains (like Hawaii), not fold mountains like the Himalayas.

27.fact: The Indian Plate is still pushing north, so the Himalayas continue to rise by a few millimetres each year.

28.correct: A day on Venus is longer than its year because Venus rotates on its axis very slowly - its single rotation (day) takes longer than one revolution around the Sun (year).

wrong.B: Venus actually revolves around the Sun faster than it spins, which is why the year is shorter.
wrong.C: Venus does rotate, just extremely slowly.
wrong.D: Venus does spin in the opposite (retrograde) direction, but the day-longer-than-year fact is due to its slow rotation.

28.fact: One Venus day equals about 243 Earth days, while a Venus year is about 225 Earth days.

29.correct: The Nagina Masjid inside the Agra Fort was built by Shah Jahan for the ladies of the royal household and has three domes.

wrong.A: The Moti Masjid in Agra Fort is a larger pearl mosque, not the private ladies' mosque described.
wrong.B: The Mina Masjid was a tiny private mosque, not the three-domed ladies' mosque.
wrong.D: The Jama Masjid at Agra stands outside the fort and was built for the public.

29.fact: Shah Jahan, who built the Taj Mahal, was a great Mughal patron of marble architecture.

30.correct: Uranus has a magnetic field that is offset from the planet's centre and tilted by about 59 degrees to its rotational axis - highly unusual among the planets.

wrong.A: Earth's magnetic field is only slightly tilted (about 11 degrees) and roughly centred.
wrong.B: Jupiter's field is strong but not tilted by ~59 degrees in this offset way.
wrong.D: Saturn's magnetic axis is almost aligned with its rotation axis.

30.fact: Uranus's strange tilted, off-centre magnetic field may be generated in its icy mantle rather than a metallic core.

31.correct: A standard Olympic boxing bout consists of three rounds of three minutes each.

wrong.B: Four 2-minute rounds is not the Olympic format.
wrong.D: Three 2-minute rounds is used in some women's formats historically, but the standard is three 3-minute rounds.

31.fact: Olympic boxing scoring is now done by judges on a 10-point must system.

32.correct: India won 22 medals in shooting at the Asian Games in Hangzhou (2023), its best-ever shooting haul at the Games.

wrong.A: 18 understates India's shooting medal count at Hangzhou.
wrong.C: 19 is short of the actual total.
wrong.D: 21 is one fewer than the correct number.

32.fact: India won 28 gold medals and over 100 total medals overall at the 2023 Asian Games.

33.correct: Blood is classified as connective tissue because its cells are suspended in a fluid matrix (plasma) and it connects and transports materials throughout the body.

wrong.A: Muscular tissue is contractile, unlike blood.
wrong.B: Nervous tissue conducts impulses; blood does not.
wrong.D: Epithelial tissue forms linings; blood is a fluid connective tissue.

33.fact: Plasma is the liquid matrix of blood, carrying red cells, white cells and platelets.

34.correct: A pollen tube growing towards the ovule (in response to chemical signals from the ovary) is an example of chemotropism - growth directed by a chemical stimulus.

wrong.A: A shoot bending towards light is phototropism.
wrong.B: A root growing downwards is geotropism (gravitropism).
wrong.D: A leaf folding on touch (as in Mimosa) is thigmonasty/seismonasty, not chemotropism.

34.fact: Tropic movements are directional growth responses to stimuli such as light, gravity, water or chemicals.

35.correct: The Chipko Movement is associated with forest conservation - villagers (notably women) hugged trees to stop them being felled.

wrong.B: It was not about air pollution.
wrong.C: It was not about wildlife trafficking.
wrong.D: It was not a river-pollution movement.

35.fact: The Chipko Movement began in the 1970s in the Garhwal Himalayas; Sunderlal Bahuguna was a key leader.

36.correct: The Rs 6,000 crore One Nation One Subscription (ONOS) initiative funds access to scholarly journals from 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2027.

wrong.A: 2032 is not the ONOS end date.

36.fact: ONOS gives students and researchers in government institutions nationwide access to international research journals.

37.correct: The British forced peasants of Maharashtra (the Deccan) to grow cotton, especially to feed Britain's textile mills during the American Civil War shortage.

wrong.B: Tea was grown on plantations in Assam and the hills, not forced on Maharashtra's peasants.
wrong.C: Jute cultivation was concentrated in Bengal.
wrong.D: Barley was not the cash crop forced in Maharashtra.

37.fact: The cotton boom and bust contributed to peasant indebtedness behind the 1875 Deccan Riots.

38.correct: The World's Parliament of Religions was held in Chicago in 1893, where Swami Vivekananda delivered his famous address.

wrong.C: 1897 is the year Vivekananda founded the Ramakrishna Mission, not the Chicago address.

38.fact: Vivekananda began his 1893 speech with 'Sisters and Brothers of America', earning a standing ovation.

39.correct: P.V. Sindhu was the first Indian woman to win an Olympic silver medal, in badminton at the 2016 Rio Olympics.

wrong.A: Aanchal Thakur is a skier who won India's first international skiing medal, not an Olympic silver.
wrong.C: Sania Mirza is a tennis great but did not win an Olympic silver.
wrong.D: Dipa Karmakar narrowly missed a medal (4th) in gymnastics at Rio 2016.

39.fact: Sindhu later won bronze at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics, becoming a two-time Olympic medallist.

40.correct: The 'Logistics Ease Across Different States (LEADS) 2024' report was unveiled by the Ministry of Commerce & Industry.

wrong.B: The Ministry of Finance does not publish the LEADS report.
wrong.C: The Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways is not the publishing ministry.
wrong.D: The Ministry of Cooperation does not release LEADS.

40.fact: LEADS assesses and ranks states/UTs on the efficiency of their logistics ecosystem.
